===== PAGE 143 =====
142 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
Large quadrupedal herbivores with elephantine bodies and ex-
tremely longnecks and tails, the sauropods are herd animals. 
They can bite, but are only really dangerous when they trample. 
Trampling automatically kills any creature weighing less than a 
ton in their path—saving throw vs death to avoid. A whole herd 
stampeding is an impressive sight, best viewed from a safe dis-
tance. In fantasy gaming environments sauropods might make 
good meat animals or beasts of burden.
Apatosaurus: Jurassic herbivore, once called brontosaurus. Di-
plodocus: Jurassic herbivore, famously long. Brachiosaurus: Ju-
rassic herbivore; long thought to be the largest land dinosaur. 
Argentinosaurus: Cretaceous herbivore, relatively recently dis -
covered, twice as massive as brachiosaurus. Camarasaurus: 
Very common Jurassic herbivore. Cetiosaurus: Jurassic herbi -
vore, earlier relative of the other types.
Special Attacks
Trample. Any sauropod listed here can trample as well as bite if 
the dinosaur can move across the victim’s location. Trampling 
requires no to-hit roll and requires the target (if the target is 
smaller than size Huge) to make a saving throw vs. death or be 
crushed to death under the sauropod’s feet.
CERATOPSIANS
CENTROSAURUS DICERATUS MONOCLONIUS PENTACERATOPS STYRACOSAURUS TRICERATOPS
FREQUENCY Rare Very Rare Uncommon Uncommon Uncommon Common
NO. 
ENCOUNTERED 1d6 1d3 1d6 10d100 1d20 1d20
SIZE Huge Huge Huge Huge Huge Huge
ALIGNMENT Neutral Neutral Neutral Neutral Neutral Neutral
MOVE 120ft 120ft 120ft 120ft 120ft 120ft
ARMOUR CLASS 3 [17] or 7 [13] 3 [17] or 7 [13] 4 [16] or 7 [13] 3 [17] or 7 [13] 2 [18] or 7 [13] 3 [17] or 7 [13]
HIT DICE 7 8 8 12 9 16
MELEE ATTACKS 1 gore (1d8) 1 gore (1d10) 1 gore (1d8) 1 gore (2d6) 1 gore (1d10) 1 gore (3d6)
SENSES Standard Standard Standard Standard Standard Standard
LAIR CHANCE Nil Nil Nil Nil Nil Nil
INTELLIGENCE Non- (0) Non- (0) Non- (0) Non- (0) Non- (0) Non- (0)
MORALE N/A N/A N/A N/A N/A N/A
LOOT Nil Nil Nil Nil Nil Nil
EXPERIENCE 200 +4/hp 500 +8/hp 400 +8/hp 1,000 +18/hp 550 +8/hp 2,000 +20/hp
Ceratopsians are quadrupedal beasts with horns and bony head 
armour. They fill the rhinoceros’ ecological niche. Ceratopsians 
are bad-tempered and prone to charge, making them among 
the most dangerous of the dinosaur herbivorous species. They 
were extremely common during the late cretaceous period. The 
best known and most common of these creatures, triceratops, 
was probably about twice as heavy as an elephant. 
Pentaceratops: Cretaceous herbivore, resembling a triceratops 
with a longer neck-frill and two extra horns protruding from 
under its eyes. Triceratops: Cretaceous herbivore. Unlike most 
other ceratopsians it does not seem to have lived in herds. Dic-
eratops: Cretaceous herbivore; extremely rare compared to tric-
eratops. Styracosaurus: Cretaceous herbivore with a distinctive 
spiky neck-frill; about half the size of triceratops. Monoclonius: 
Cretaceous herbivore with only one horn. Centrosaurus: Creta-
ceous herbivore that seems to have lived in very large herds.
Special Features
Segmented Armour Class. Ceratopsians have two AC scores: one 
for their armoured heads, the other for their bodies.
Special Attacks
Stomp. If a ceratopsian attacks (other than with a charge) and 
exceeds its required “to hit” roll by 4 or more, it has knocked 
down its target and will stomp on it for an additional 1d10 
points of damage.
Charge. If a ceratopsian charges and hits, it inflicts triple dam -
age.
===== PAGE 144 =====
 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 143

A miscellany of other kinds of dinosaur, including some (ank -
lyosaurus and stegosaurus) with bony, armoured backs. Both 
these creatures have an effective attack by clubbing with their 
tails. GMs who are fans of Jurassic Park may wish to increase the 
velociraptor’s intelligence score to “semi-” or even “low”.
Anklyosaurus: Huge Cretaceous herbivore. Ceratosaurus: Large 
Jurassic carnivore. Plateosaurus: Large Triassic herbivore. Igua-
nodon: Large Cretaceous herbivore. Stegosaurus: Huge Jurassic 
herbivore. Velociraptor: Large Cretaceous carnivore.
Special Attacks
Velociraptor Rake. If a velociraptor hits with its bite, it can bring 
its rear claws into action as well as its foreclaws. It gains an 
immediate follow-up attack with the 2 rear claws (2d4), and 
can continue to attack with the rear claws for as long as it con-
tinues to attack the target it bit (as well as normal biting and 
foreclaws).
OTHER DINOSAURS II
COMPSOGNATHUS DEINONYCHUS DIMETRODON DILOPHOSAURUS EUPARKERIA ORNITHOLESTES
FREQUENCY Common Uncommon Uncommon Uncommon Uncommon Common
NO. 
ENCOUNTERED 8d4 1 or 2d4 3d6 1d4+1 5d8 5d4

144 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
Dimetrodon is technically a synapsid, and is anach-
ronistic here by quite a large number of millions 
of years, being more separated from Tyrannosau -
rus in time than 21st century humans are. It had a 
huge “sail” on its back that may have helped it reg-
ulate its temperature. GMs who are fans of Juras-
sic Park should note the dilophosaurus presented 
there was a result of quite a lot of artistic license.
Compsognathus: Small Jurassic carnivore. Deinon-
ychus: Large Cretaceous carnivore. Dimetrodon: 
Large Permian carnivore. Euparkeria: Small Trias -
sic carnivore. Ornitholestes: Medium Jurassic carni-
vore. Dilophosaurus: Large Jurassic carnivore.
MARINE DINOSAURS

EXPERIENCE 475 +10/hp 1,000 +12/hp 2,000 +16/hp 1,200 +16/hp 1,000 +12/hp 5,000 +25/hp
None of these creatures are technically dinosaurs, though all 
are found in “Lost World” areas. Dunkleosteus can swallow prey 
whole on a roll of “20” “to hit” and actually preceded the dino -
saurs by many millions of years. Elasmosaurus can also swallow 
whole, needing only 18–20 on its “to hit” roll to do so but oth -
erwise as dunkleosteus. Mosasaurus filled the same ecological 
niche as sharks. Plesiosaurs may have preferred smaller prey 
than a man but would still be dangerous. Nothosaurs were am-
phibious, like huge seals. Archelon was a kind of gigantic turtle.
Dunkleosteus: Formerly known as dinichthys; Huge Devonian 
carnivore. Elasmosaurus: Huge Cretaceous carnivore. Mosasau-
rus: Huge Cretaceous carnivore. Plesiosaurus: Huge Jurassic car-
nivore. Nothosaurus: Large Triassic carnivore. Archelon: Huge 
Cretaceous carnivore.
Special Attacks
Dunkleosteus can swallow prey whole on a to-hit roll of 20. 
Elasmosaurus can also swallow whole, needing only 18–20 on 
its “to hit” roll.
Swallow Whole. On a bite attack that rolls a natural 20, the dino-
saur swallows the victim. If the victim is not rescued, they will 
die at the end of 6 rounds spent in the creature’s stomach. 
It is possible for the victim to cut their way out (the inside 
is armour class 10 [10]). For each round the victim is inside, 
subtract 1 from their damage that they inflict. This penalty is 
cumulative for each round, so after four rounds the damage 
inflicted is at -4.

Pterodactyl was really several different kinds of creature, here 
lumped together. Quetzalcoatlus is assumed to have filled the 
raptor niche, while the other flying dinosaurs are assumed to 
have been found in flocks.
Pteranodon: Large Cretaceous carnivore. Pterodactyl: Small Ju-
rassic carnivore. Archaeopteryx: Small Jurassic carnivore; no big-
ger than an ordinary bird. Quetzalcoatlus: Huge Cretaceous car-
nivore. Rhamphorhynchus: Large Jurassic carnivore.

146 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
DISENCHANTER
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d2
SIZE Medium (5ft at shoulder)
ALIGNMENT Neutral
MOVE 120ft
ARMOUR CLASS 5 [15]
HIT DICE 5
MELEE ATTACKS 1 (special)
SENSES Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE Nil
INTELLIGENCE Average (8-10)
MORALE 70
LOOT Nil
EXPERIENCE 225 +5/hp
The disenchanter resembles a rather bizarre cross between a 
dromedary camel and a cow, with a muscular prehensile snout 
stuck on for good measure. Colouration is a shimmery electric 
blue, and the entire creature appears to be somewhat translu -
cent and difficult for the eye to focus upon.
The disenchanter is a thaumivore, feeding on magical power. 
It sucks the dweomer from enchanted objects by attaching its 
snout to them with a successful “to hit” roll. 
The disenchanter can only be hit by magic weapons but doing 
so will not affect the dweomer of the weapon; only the snout 
has the ability to disenchant items. Even the wisest and most 
learned of sages know nothing about the ecology of the disen -
chanter or why it seems to be able to sustain itself only with 
magic items.
Immunities and Resistances
Immune to Mundane Weapons. This creature is immune to pierc-
ing, blunt, and slashing damage from weapons and unarmed 
attacks, unless the weapon causing that damage has a magical 
bonus of +1 or more.
Special Attacks
Disenchant Item. One touch (a normal to-hit roll) is all it takes 
for the disenchanter’s snout to render any magic item (except 
major artifacts) completely inert. The creature has no other at-
tacks and causes no physical damage of any kind. The muscu -
lar snout can be extended as much as 5ft and the disenchanter 
is capable of detecting relative strengths of magic enchant -
ment, knowing, for instance, that plate mail +5 will be a tastier 
morsel than a mere +1 ring of protection.
Unarmed Combat. Disenchanters cannot be overborne or grap -
pled, and do not employ these tactics. 
DJINN (GENIE)
DJINNI NOBLE DJINNI
FREQUENCY Very rare Very rare
NO. 
ENCOUNTERED 1 1
SIZE Large (8ft) Large (8ft)
ALIGNMENT Chaotic good Chaotic good
MOVE 90ft; 240ft flying 
(AA:VI)
90ft; 240ft flying 
(AA:VI)
ARMOUR CLASS 4 [16] 4 [16]
HIT DICE 7+3 10
MELEE ATTACKS 1 slap (2d8) 1 slap (3d8)
SENSES Infravision 60ft Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE Nil Nil
INTELLIGENCE Average (8-10) to 
high (13-14)
Average (8-10) to 
high (13-14)
MORALE 85 95
LOOT Nil Nil
EXPERIENCE 650 +10/hp 1200 +13/hp
Djinn (singular “djinni”) come from the Elemental Plane of Air 
and are quite magical. They can travel the elemental planes and 
the Astral Plane, as well as the Prime Material. If subdued, they 
can be enslaved for 1,001 days. Once their service has expired, 
they are free and may not become enslaved again by the same 
creature.
A djinni can carry up to 600  lbs without tiring, either flying or 
walking. A djinni is able to carry double the weight on foot but 
only for about thirty minutes. A djinni will require an hour’s rest 
for every 30 minutes of carrying.
Immunities and Resistances
If any attack is made on the djinni by an airborne attacker, the 
attack will be at -1 “to hit” and -1 to damage.
Special Features
Spell-like Abilities. 
At Will: Djinns can become invisible or enter gaseous form at 
will.
In gaseous form, the creature takes on a translucent, gaseous 
consistency and floats at 30ft per round. This speed can be dif -
ferent depending on natural wind speed in the environment 
or due to the effects of wind–altering spells. While in gaseous 
form, the creature can flow below doors and other small spac -
es not sealed airtight. Although magical lightning and fire do 
the gaseous form full damage, the creature is impervious to all 
other attacks.
===== PAGE 148 =====
 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 147
1/day: Djinn can perform any of the following abilities once per 
day: create food for up to 12 people; create water or wine for 
up to 12 people; create up to 16 cubicft of cloth, 9 cubicft of 
wood, or 100 pounds of metal (the metal is not permanent 
and will disappear within a day); create an illusion with both 
sight and sound that will act on its own without being con -
trolled. The illusion will last until it is dispelled or touched. 
Three Wishes (Noble Djinn only).  If subdued, a noble djinni need 
only grant three wishes to be released from service. The djinni 
can effectively cast three wish spells per day, but if the djinni 
is angry at the person asking for the wishes the wording of the 
request should be very specific, because the djinni will comply 
with the least beneficial interpretation possible.
Special Attacks
Once per day, djinn can assume the form of a whirlwind 7ft tall, 
3ft across at the top, and 1ft across at the bottom. This lasts 
for an entire round and does 2d6 hit points of damage to all 
creatures caught in its path (3d6 for a noble djinn). Any creature 
with less than 2 hit dice are killed instantly. The djinni can move 
30ft in one round while in whirlwind form, but can turn while 
moving, to hit a large number of opponents.
Unarmed Combat. Djinn can be targeted with grappling or over-
bearing attacks, and under some circumstances they may use 
these tactics. If not fended off, they score an unarmed hit with 
a 2+ on 1d20. Hit Mods: ATK/DEF +10/-10. Overbear Results 
Mods: ATK/DEF +4/-4. Grappling Result Mods: ATK/DEF +2/-2.
Language
Djinn speak the language of the elemental plane of air, but they 
also have a limited form of telepathy that enables them to com-
municate with any intelligent creature.
DOG
WAR DOG 
(MASTIFF)
ORDINARY OR WILD 
DOG
FREQUENCY Uncommon Common
NO. 
ENCOUNTERED Varies 4d4
SIZE Medium Small
ALIGNMENT Neutral Neutral
MOVE 120ft 150ft
ARMOUR CLASS 6 [14] (or by 
 armour type) 7 [13]
HIT DICE 2+2 1
MELEE ATTACKS 1 bite (2d4) 1 bite (1d4)
SENSES Keen smell Keen smell
LAIR CHANCE Nil Nil
INTELLIGENCE Semi- (2-4) Semi- (2-4)
MORALE N/A 50
LOOT Nil Nil
EXPERIENCE 50 +2/hp 10 +1/hp
War Dogs are usually large mastiffs or pit bulls, trained to fight 
or guard a location. They are typically armoured in leather. They 
do not check morale and are trained not to accept food from 
strangers.
Most dogs are in the “Ordinary or Wild” category, including most 
guard dogs.
All dogs have a 25% chance to detect the presence of an in -
visible person by smell. How the dog reacts to it depends on 
training. A war dog will usually be trained to attempt an attack 
(still with a -4 to hit), a guard dog may be trained to bark at any-
thing suspicious, and a relatively untrained dog may just follow 
the invisible person’s scent until something causes it to react 
differently.
Unarmed Combat.  Dogs cannot be targeted with grappling or 
overbearing attacks, but war dogs may be trained to overbear. 
If not fended off, they score an unarmed hit with a 2+ on 1d20. 
Hit Mods: ATK/DEF +8/na. Overbear Results Mods: ATK/DEF 
+2/-0. Grappling Result Mods: ATK/DEF na/na.

===== PAGE 149 =====
148 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
DOPPELGÄNGER
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 3d4
SIZE Medium
ALIGNMENT Neutral
MOVE 90ft
ARMOUR CLASS 5 [15]
HIT DICE 4
MELEE ATTACKS 1 bite (1d12)
SENSES Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE 20%
INTELLIGENCE Very (11-12)
MORALE 65
LOOT Horde 5
EXPERIENCE 285 +3/hp
Doppelgängers are strange beings that are able to take on the 
forms of those they encounter, including the appearance of 
equipment and any clothing the victim is wearing. In its natural 
form, a doppelgänger is a 5ft to 6ft tall humanoid, slender with 
gangly limbs and half-formed features. Its flesh is pale and hair-
less, and its large, bulging eyes are yellow with slitted pupils. 
The creature will assume its natural form upon death.
Doppelgängers make excellent use of their natural mimicry to 
stage ambushes, bait traps, and infiltrate society. Their classic 
tactic in a dungeon is to take the form of a member of an adven-
turing party, dispatch them, and take their place amongst the 
group until a good time presents itself to kill them or rob them.
Initiative and Surprise
Stealth. Doppelgangers surprise with a roll of 1–4 on 1d6.
Immunities and Resistances
Immunities. Doppelgangers are immune to the effects of charm 
and sleep spells.
Improved Saving Throw.  Doppelgängers make saving throws as 
a 10HD monster.
Special Features
ESP. Doppelgangers can use ESP (as per the spell) at will.
Natural Disguise.  A doppelgänger can assume any humanoid 
form between 4ft to 8ft tall, but the creature must first see the 
victim to be copied. Any doppelgänger’s attempt at physical 
disguise is only faulty 10% of the time, and its knowledge of the 
victim is facilitated by the doppelgänger’s innate ESP ability.
Unarmed Combat.  Doppelgangers can be targeted with grap -
pling or overbearing attacks, and under some circumstances 
they may use these tactics. If not fended off, they score an un-
armed hit with a 2+ on 1d20. Hit Mods: ATK/DEF +6/-6. Over -
bear Results Mods: ATK/DEF +0/-0. Grappling Result Mods: 
ATK/DEF +0/-0.
DRACOLISK
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d2
SIZE Huge
ALIGNMENT Chaotic evil
MOVE 90ft; 150ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS 3 [17]
HIT DICE 7+3
MELEE ATTACKS 3 (1d6/1d6/3d4)
SENSES Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE 60%
INTELLIGENCE Low to average (5-10)
MORALE 85
LOOT Hoard 3 and 9
EXPERIENCE 1,000 +10/hp
Dracolisks are surmised to be the result of the mating of a black 
dragon and a basilisk, though rumours persist of the existence 
of other sorts. These fearsome creatures most resemble their 
draconic sires, being armoured with thick black scales and hav-
ing the horns and sharp toothed maws of their forebears; they 
also have six powerful clawed limbs and two large wings. 
Although capable of flight, dracolisks are limited to only short 
durations, ten or twenty minutes at the most, before they have 
to rest, so they principally use this form of movement to swoop 
down upon or escape from foes. 
Special Features
Additionally, anybody unfortunate enough to be within 30ft of 
a dracolisk and meet its gaze must make a saving throw vs pet-
rifaction or be turned to stone. As with its basilisk parent, the 
gaze attack of the dracolisk extends into the astral and ethereal 
planes and those who seek to avoid looking directly at the beast 
attack with a -4 penalty.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon (3/day). Up to thrice a day, a dracolisk is capable 
of shooting an acid stream out of its mouth up to 30ft and with 
a width of 5ft; this causes 4d6 damage to anybody caught in 
its path, though a successful saving throw vs breath weapons 
reduces this by half.
Unarmed Combat. Dracolisks cannot be overborne or grappled, 
and do not employ these tactics. 
Language
If a dracolisk speaks a language at all, it is usually draconic, 
though it may be that some can understand other languages.
===== PAGE 150 =====
 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 149
DRAGONS
Dragons are, in many ways, the archetypal adversaries in OS -
RIC-compatible games. Some dragons are weak enough that even 
a relatively low level party can have a chance to fight a dragon—
while others are powerful enough to pose a challenge even to a 
high-level party. Even parties of very high average level may well 
struggle to defeat a mated pair, or family group, of elder dragons!
Dragons come in various colours. In many campaigns, these co-
lours are literal—fire-breathing dragons really do always have 
red scales—but in others, they may be metaphorical, allowing 
(for example) a green or brown “red” dragon. Some campaigns 
also allow the possibility of dragons with more than 8 hp per die 
and dragons with d10, d12 or even larger Hit Dice are possible.
There are some traits that are generally common to all dragons, 
no matter the colour or alignment. First, if issued with a formal 
challenge, a dragon will sometimes agree to fight until subdued 
rather than to the death. The GM should rule about the likeli -
hood of the dragon’s acceptance and the conditions that apply, 
since these will depend on the nature of the challenge and the 
alignment of the dragon. Second, dragons grow more powerful 
as they age (see Age Categories, below). Dragons do not roll dice 
for their hit points or breath weapons; these are determined by 
age category, which also influences a saving throw bonus and 
the ability to perceive invisible opponents. Third, dragons radi -
ate an aura of fear (as the 4th level magic-user spell) affecting 
creatures of less than 5th level unless they save vs magic. 
Unarmed Combat. Dragons cannot be overborne or grappled. A 
dragon might choose to overbear. Stats for unarmed combat 
vary by size, so the GM will have to calculate those. (Although 
the to-hit number will be 2+).
Size and Hit Dice
Most dragon types have 3 possibilities for their hit dice (Exam -
ple: Black dragons have 6 to 8 HD). Roll a d8 to determine a 
dragon’s hit dice, with 1-2 indicating the smallest (a black drag-
on with 6 hit dice), 3-7 indicating the middle number (a black 
dragon with 7 hit dice), and an 8 indicating the highest number 
(a black dragon with 8 hit dice).
For a dragon with the smallest HD number (our 6HD black drag-
on), the dragon’s size will be approximately 4ft of body length 
(not including the neck or tail) per age category (see below). The 
middle HD number will have a body length of 5ft per age cat -
egory, and the highest HD number will have a body length of 
6ft per age category. The tails and necks of dragons vary, but 
a dragon’s full length is usually about 3-4 times its body length. 
Any dragon with a body length of 9ft or less is considered Medi-
um, a body length of 10-20ft is Large, and a body length larger 
than 20ft is Huge.
 Age Categories
Roll 1d8 to determine the age category of a randomly-encoun -
tered dragon. Dragon hit points and breath weapon damage 
are determined by the dragon’s age category, and older drag -
ons have a bonus to all saving throws.
 AGE CATEGORY  AGE HIT POINTS 
PER HIT DIE
SAVING THROW 
BONUS
RADIUS OF  INVISIBILITY 
DETECTION
1 Hatchling, Younger than 6 years. 1 +0 10ft
2 Young dragon, 6 years to 15 years 2 +0 20ft
3 Adolescent dragon, 16 years to 25 years 3 +0 30ft
4 Young adult dragon, 26 to 50 years 4 +0 40ft
5 Adult dragon, 51 to 100 years 5 +1 50ft
6 Old dragon, 101 to 200 years 6 +2 60ft
7 Very old dragon, 201 to 400 years 7 +3 70ft
8 Ancient, 401 years and older 8 +4 80ft

DRAGON, BLACK
FREQUENCY Uncommon
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Chaotic evil
MOVE 120ft; 240ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS 3 [17]
HIT DICE 6 to 8
MELEE ATTACKS 2 claws (1d4) and 1 bite (3d6)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect 
invisibility by age category
LAIR CHANCE 30%
INTELLIGENCE Average (8-10)
MORALE 75, 80, or 85 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
Viking champions such as Ragnar Lodbrok and Sigurd the 
Volsung fought fearsome dragons that spewed a corrosive ven-
om, deadly to the touch. These dragons are the deadly black 
dragons. Black dragons are found mostly in swamps, marshes 
and deep, dark caves.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon.  A black dragon encountered in its lair has a 
50% chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If 
the dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon. Only 10% of black dragons are spellcasters. 
If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user, half the dragon’s 
age category in level (1-2=1, 3-4=2, etc.).
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon.  Black dragon breath is a stream of corrosive, 
acidic venom, 50ft long×5ft wide. It does corrosion damage 
equal to the dragon’s (uninjured) hit points to everyone in the 
area of effect, but a saving throw vs dragon breath reduces this 
amount to half damage. The dragon may use this breath weap-
on up to three times per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 30% 
chance that a black dragon will also be able to speak common.
DRAGON, BLUE
FREQUENCY Rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Lawful evil
MOVE 90ft; 240ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS 2 [18]
HIT DICE 8 to 10
MELEE ATTACKS 2 claws (1d6) and 1 bite (3d8)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect 
invisibility by age category
LAIR CHANCE 50%
INTELLIGENCE Very (11-12)
MORALE 85, 90, or 95 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8, Cache 5
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
Deserts and dry wastes are home to the deadly blue dragons. 
These creatures usually dwell in vast caves or deserted towers 
in arid lands.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon. A blue dragon encountered in its lair has a 30% 
chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If the 
dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon. Roughly 30% of blue dragons are spellcast-
ers. If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user with a level 
equal to the dragon’s age category.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. Blue dragon breath is a bolt of lightning, 100ft 
long×5ft wide. It does electrical damage equal to the dragon’s 
(uninjured) hit points to everyone in the area of effect, but a sav-
ing throw vs dragon breath reduces this amount to half dam -
age. The dragon may use this breath weapon up to three times 
per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 60% 
chance that a blue dragon will also be able to speak common.
===== PAGE 152 =====
 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 151
DRAGON, BRASS
FREQUENCY Uncommon
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Chaotic good or Chaotic neutral
MOVE 120ft; 240ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS 3 [17]
HIT DICE 6 to 8
MELEE ATTACKS 2 claws (1d4) and 1 bite (4d4)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect invisibility by 
age category
LAIR CHANCE 25%
INTELLIGENCE High (13-14)
MORALE 75, 80, or 85 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
The blue dragons of the desert must contend with their deadly 
rivals, the brass dragons, who love the same arid caves. The 
brass dragons are not the vicious killers that the blue dragons 
are, although their good nature is sometimes overcome with 
doses of greed and self-interest.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon.  A brass dragon encountered in its lair has a 
50% chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If 
the dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon. Roughly 30% of brass dragons are spellcast-
ers. If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user, half the 
dragon’s age category in level (1-2=1, 3-4=2, etc.).
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. Brass dragon breath is either a cone of sleep-in-
ducing gas (70ft×20ft), or a cloud of fear gas (40ft×50ft,×20ft), 
at the dragon’s choice of how to breathe. The dragon may use 
either breath weapon up to a total of three times per day. 
A saving throw vs. dragon breath negates the effect of the 
breath. Saves against a 6HD dragon’s breath are at a +2, but 
they are made at -2 against that of an 8HD dragon. The dura -
tion of the effect is 10 rounds.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 30% 
chance that a brass dragon will also be able to speak common.
DRAGON, BRONZE
FREQUENCY Rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Lawful good
MOVE 90ft; 240ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS 2 [18]
HIT DICE 8 to 10
MELEE ATTACKS 3 (1d6/1d6/4d6)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect invisibility by 
age category
LAIR CHANCE 45%
INTELLIGENCE Exceptional (15-16)
MORALE 85, 90, or 95 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8, Cache 5 and 6
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
The reclusive bronze dragons live in caverns in temperate cli -
mates. They are interested in the ways of humanity and often 
use shape-changing powers to watch, observe and help peo -
ple. They prefer to have their lairs in places isolated from prying 
eyes, however, and thus often place their entrances in lakes or 
ponds. They have found that their treasure hordes are tempting 
targets for evil or even neutral adventurers.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon. A bronze dragon encountered in its lair has a 
25% chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If 
the dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon. Roughly 60% of bronze dragons are spell -
casters. If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user with a 
level equal to the dragon’s age category.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. Bronze dragon breath is (at the dragon’s choice) 
one of two types. The first is a bolt of lightning, 100ft long and 
5ft wide. It does damage equal to the dragon’s hit points (but a 
save vs dragon breath halves this amount). The other option is 
to breathe a cloud of repulsion gas (20ft ×30ft×30ft) which re -
quires all subjects to save vs dragon breath or be repulsed for 
6 minutes (as per the level 6 magic-user spell). The dragon may 
use breath weapons up to three times per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 60% 
chance that a bronze dragon will also be able to speak common.
===== PAGE 153 =====
152 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
DRAGON, COPPER
FREQUENCY Uncommon to rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Chaotic good
MOVE 90ft; 240ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS 1 [19]
HIT DICE 7 to 9
MELEE ATTACKS 2 claws (1d4) and 1 bite (3d6+2)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect invisibility by 
age category
LAIR CHANCE 35%
INTELLIGENCE High (13-14)
MORALE 80, 85, or 90 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8, Cache 5
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
Copper dragons live in wild and rugged places. They do not seek 
out the company of humans and are quite reclusive. However, 
they have a strong sense of morality, and will occasionally assist 
humans in need. When they do, they are happy to accept any 
reward offered, for they are as greedy as most other dragon 
types.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon. A copper dragon encountered in its lair has a 
40% chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If 
the dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon. Roughly 40% of bronze dragons are spell -
casters. If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user with a 
level equal to the dragon’s age category.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. Copper dragon breath is a blast of acid, like a 
black dragon’s breath, that does damage equal to the dragon’s 
hit points (but a save vs dragon breath halves this amount); or 
it may breathe a cloud of slowing gas (20ft ×30ft×30ft) which 
requires all subjects to save vs breath weapon or be slowed (as 
the spell) for 6 minutes. The dragon may use breath weapons 
up to three times per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 45% 
chance that a copper dragon will also be able to speak common.
DRAGON, GOLD
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d3
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Lawful good
MOVE 120ft; 300ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS -2 [22]
HIT DICE 10 to 12
MELEE ATTACKS 2 claws (1d8) and 1 bite (6d6)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect invisibility by 
age category
LAIR CHANCE 65%
INTELLIGENCE Genius (17-18)
MORALE 95, 100 or 105 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8, Cache 4, 5, and 6
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
Often shaped like the sinuous dragons of Asian myth, gold 
dragons epitomise law and goodness, and are typically just 
and kindly. They are interested in the ways of man and often 
use shape-changing powers to watch, observe and help peo -
ple. They prefer to have their lairs in places isolated from prying 
eyes, however, and thus often place their entrances in lakes or 
ponds. They have found that their treasure hordes are tempting 
targets for evil or even neutral adventurers.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon. A gold dragon encountered in its lair has a 10% 
chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If the 
dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon. Roughly 90% of gold dragons are spellcast -
ers. If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user with a level 
equal to the dragon’s age category.
Polymorph. A gold dragon can cast polymorph self as an innate 
ability three times per day.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. Gold dragon breath is a blast of fire (like that of 
a red dragon) or a cloud of poison gas (like a green dragon), at 
the dragon’s option. The dragon may use breath weapons up 
to three times per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 90% 
chance that a gold dragon will also be able to speak common.
===== PAGE 154 =====
 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 153
DRAGON, GREEN
FREQUENCY Rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Lawful evil
MOVE 90ft; 240ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS 2 [18]
HIT DICE 7 to 9
MELEE ATTACKS 2 claws (1d6) and 1 bite (2d10)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect 
invisibility by age category
LAIR CHANCE 40%
INTELLIGENCE Average (8-10) to very (11-12)
MORALE 80, 85, or 90 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
Particularly renowned for their foul temper, which is said to be 
even more venomous than their breath, green dragons prefer 
to live in caverns or ruins in or near forests.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon. A green dragon encountered in its lair has a 
40% chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If 
the dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon.  Roughly 20% of green dragons are spell -
casters. If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user with a 
level equal to the dragon’s age category.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. Green dragon breath is a cloud of toxic gas, 50ft 
long, 40ft wide, and 30ft high. It does poison damage equal to 
the dragon’s (uninjured) hit points to everyone in the area of 
effect, but a saving throw vs dragon breath reduces this amount 
to half damage. The dragon may use this breath weapon up to 
three times per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 45% 
chance that a green dragon will also be able to speak common.
DRAGON, RED
FREQUENCY Rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Chaotic evil
MOVE 90ft; 240ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS -1 [21]
HIT DICE 9 to 11
MELEE ATTACKS 3 (1d8/1d8/3d10)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect 
invisibility by age category
LAIR CHANCE 60%
INTELLIGENCE Exceptional (15-16)
MORALE 90, 95, or 100 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8, Cache 5 and 6
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
The archetypal red dragon is Smaug from The Hobbit.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon. A red dragon encountered in its lair has a 20% 
chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If the 
dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon. Roughly 40% of red dragons are spellcast -
ers. If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user with a level 
equal to the dragon’s age category.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. Red dragon breath is a cone of flame, 90ft long 
and 15ft radius at the base, emanating from the mouth of the 
creature. It does fire damage equal to the dragon’s (uninjured) 
hit points to everyone in the area of effect, but a saving throw vs 
dragon breath reduces this amount to half damage. The dragon 
may use this breath weapon up to three times per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is an 80% 
chance that a red dragon will also be able to speak common.
===== PAGE 155 =====
154 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
DRAGON, SILVER
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Lawful good
MOVE 90ft; 240ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS -1 [21]
HIT DICE 9 to 11
MELEE ATTACKS 3 (1d6/1d6/5d6)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect 
invisibility by age category
LAIR CHANCE 55%
INTELLIGENCE Exceptional (15-16)
MORALE 90, 95, or 100 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 8, Cache 6
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
Dwelling in high places such as mountaintops or among the 
clouds, like gold dragons, silver dragons epitomise law and 
goodness. They are interested in the ways of humankind and 
often use shape-changing powers to watch, observe and help 
people. They prefer to have their lairs in places isolated from 
prying eyes, however, and thus often place their entrances in 
lakes or ponds. They have found that their treasure hordes are 
tempting targets for evil or even neutral adventurers.
Special Features
Polymorph. A silver dragon can cast polymorph self as an innate 
ability three times per day.
Sleeping Dragon.  A silver dragon encountered in its lair has a 
15% chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If 
the dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon.  Roughly 75% of silver dragons who can 
speak are spellcasters. If it can cast spells, it does so as a mag-
ic-user with a level equal to the dragon’s age category.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. Silver dragon breath is a blast of frost (like that 
of a white dragon) or a cloud of paralysing gas (like a green 
dragon except those who fail their saves are paralysed for 
3d4 turns), at the dragon’s option. The dragon may use breath 
weapons up to three times per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 75% 
chance that a silver dragon will also be able to speak common.
DRAGON, WHITE
FREQUENCY Uncommon
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Varies by age
ALIGNMENT Chaotic evil
MOVE 120ft; 300ft flying (AA:II)
ARMOUR CLASS 3 [17]
HIT DICE 5 to 7
MELEE ATTACKS 2 claws (1d4) and 1 bite (2d8)
SENSES Infravision 60ft, Detect 
invisibility by age category
LAIR CHANCE 30%
INTELLIGENCE Low (5-7)
MORALE 70, 75, or 80 (by HD)
LOOT Hoard 5, Cache 1 and 5
EXPERIENCE Varies by hit dice and age
Almost the antithesis of the fiery red dragon is the frost-rimed 
white dragon of the frigid north. Although lesser than their red 
cousins in size, power and intelligence, they are at least as evil.
Special Features
Sleeping Dragon.  A white dragon encountered in its lair has a 
60% chance to be sleeping when the adventurers approach. If 
the dragon is asleep, there is a 1 in 6 chance that it will awaken 
from the mere presence of adventurers. Dragons also some -
times feign sleep.
Spellcasting Dragon. Only 5% of white dragons are spellcasters. 
If it can cast spells, it does so as a magic-user, half the dragon’s 
age category in level (age category 1-2= level 1, category 3-4= 
level 2, etc.).
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon. White dragon breath is a cone of deadly frost, 
50ft long and 25ft diameter at the base. It does damage equal 
to the dragon’s (uninjured) hit points to all in the area of effect, 
but a save vs breath weapons reduces the damage to half. The 
dragon may use this breath weapon up to three times per day.
Language
All dragons speak the draconic tongue, and there is a 20% 
chance that a white dragon will also be able to speak common.

 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 155
DRAGON TURTLE
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1
SIZE Huge
ALIGNMENT Neutral
MOVE 30ft; 90ft swimming
ARMOUR CLASS 0 [20]
HIT DICE 12 to 15
MELEE ATTACKS 2 claws (2d6) and 1 bite (4d8)
SENSES Standard, Detect invisible 
25%, 35%, 45% or 55% by HD
LAIR CHANCE 5%
INTELLIGENCE Very (11-12)
MORALE Depends on HD

EXPERIENCE
12HD: 2750 +16/hp 
13HD: 3300 +17/HP 
14HD: 3950 +18/HP 
15HD: 4600 +19/HP
Dragon turtles are not true dragons, but they are clearly relat -
ed, looking much like a dragon with a turtle shell.
Special Attacks
Breath Weapon.  Dragon turtles can breathe a cone of steam, 
60ft long and 40ft wide at the base. It does damage equal to 
the dragon turtle’s (uninjured) hit points to all in the area of 
effect, but a save vs breath weapons reduces the damage to 
half. The dragon turtle may use this breath weapon up to three 
times per day.
Unarmed Combat. Dragon turtles cannot be overborne or grap -
pled, and do not employ these tactics. 
Language
Dragon turtles speak their own language and that of the Neu -
tral alignment. There is a 20% chance that a dragon turtle will 
also be able to speak common.
DRYAD
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d6
SIZE Medium
ALIGNMENT Neutral
MOVE 120ft
ARMOUR CLASS 10 [10]
HIT DICE 2
MELEE ATTACKS 1 (1d2)
SENSES Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE 10%
INTELLIGENCE High (13-14)
MORALE 55
LOOT Individual 4 (x100), Cache 3 (x10)
EXPERIENCE 100 +3/hp.
Dryads are tree sprites who dwell in the most remote locations. 
Dryads are female, although male “hamadryads” are encoun -
tered just as often. Dryads all have a home tree that houses 
their spirits (if this tree is killed, the dryad dies). They are nev -
er far (400 yards, or thereabouts) from this spirit-tree. Dryads 
are non-violent and will never attack unless they have no other 
choice. 
Dryads and hamadryads, as fey creatures, have a tendency to 
kidnap humans using their charm ability. If successful, there is a 
50% chance the victim will never be seen again. If they do return 
to civilisation, it will be at least 1d4 years later.
Immunities and Resistances
Magic Resistance (50%).  Any magic spell or effect has a 50% 
chance (at caster level 11) to have no effect on this creature; 
but if it does, the magic takes full effect.
Special Features
Spell-Like Abilities
At Will: Dryads can dimension door by stepping into any nearby 
tree, returning to their own spirit-tree. 
3/day: A dryad can cast a charm person spell thrice per day.
Unarmed Combat. Dryads and hamadryads can make grappling 
or overbearing attacks, but ordinarily don’t. If not fended off, 
they score an unarmed hit with a 2+ on 1d20. Hit Mods: ATK/
DEF +8/-8. Overbear Results Mods: ATK/DEF +0/+0. Grappling 
Result Mods: ATK/DEF +0/+0.
Language
Dryads have their own language as well as elven, pixie, sprite, 
and they can also speak with animals.

===== PAGE 157 =====
156 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
DWARF
FREQUENCY Common
NO. ENCOUNTERED 4d100
SIZE Small (4ft)
ALIGNMENT Lawful good
MOVE 60ft
ARMOUR CLASS 4 [16]
HIT DICE 1
MELEE ATTACKS By weapon
SENSES Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE 50%
INTELLIGENCE Very (11-12)
MORALE 50
LOOT Individual 4 (x5); in lair Hoard 7, Cache 
3 (x20) and 4
EXPERIENCE 30 +1/hp
Dwarfs are sturdy demi-humans who live in extended clans. 
They typically make their homes in rocky hills. Dwarfs are led 
by higher level leader types (additional to the number encoun -
tered die roll) as follows:
• For every 40 dwarfs: 2nd to 6th level fighter (1d6, 1 = 2nd lev-
el, 2–6 = level indicated by die roll).
• If over 160 dwarfs: 1 6th level fighter (chief) & 1 4th level fight-
er (lieutenant).
• If over 200 dwarfs: 1 fighter/cleric (of 3rd to 6th level as fighter 
and 4th to 7th level as cleric).
• If over 320 dwarfs: 1 8th level fighter, 1 7th level fighter, 1 6th 
level fighter/ 7th level cleric.
• If encountered in their lair: 2d6 fighters (2nd to 5th level), 2d4 
fighter/ clerics (2nd to 4th level), plus 1d100 noncombatant 
dwarfs.
Higher level fighters and fighter/clerics have a 10% chance (per 
level) of having magic armour or weapons.
Dwarfs are typically armoured in chain mail and carry shields. 
Axes and hammers are their weapons of choice. Dwarfs receive 
a +1 on “to hit” rolls vs goblins, orcs, and hobgoblins due to their 
intense hatred of these ancestries. When in melee with giants, 
trolls, and ogres, these opponents must fight at -4 due to their 
size and the skill of dwarfs in fighting such opponents.
Description: Dwarfs grow long beards, and usually favour dark, 
sombre colours for their clothing, with flashes of bright colours. 
They are short but have stocky builds and are known for their 
great strength. In some campaign worlds, they may wear kilts.
Mountain Dwarfs: These dwarfs are similar to their cousins, the 
hill dwarfs (described above), except they have 1d8+1 hit dice 
and they are slightly taller (4.5ft tall).
Immunities and Resistances
Saving Throw Bonus (Poison and Magic). Dwarfs are a hardy peo-
ple, and save vs poison and magic as if they were 4 levels (or 
hit dice) higher than their actual level.
Special Features
Ancestral Features. Dwarfs have the same ancestral features as 
dwarven player characters. 
Unarmed Combat. For a dwarf wearing chainmail: If not fended 
off, they score an unarmed hit with a 14+ on 1d20. Hit Mods: 
ATK/DEF +4/-0. Overbear Results Mods: ATK/DEF -2/+2. Grap -
pling Result Mods: ATK/DEF -2/+2.
Language
Dwarfs speak common, goblin, gnome, kobold, and orc in addi-
tion to their own tongue.

GIANT MORAY EELS
Giant morays are saltwater dwellers with a nasty temper and 
a mouth full of teeth. Though they will generally keep to them -
selves if not molested, giant moray eels are often found in the 
service of locathah (q.v.) as both battle mounts and guards. Gi -
ant morays encountered in those circumstances are extremely 
aggressive. On rare occasions, a giant moray eel will be found 
in fresh water.
===== PAGE 158 =====
 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 157
GIANT ELECTRIC EELS
Electric eels are non-aggressive freshwater dwellers, preferring 
warm water. Rumours persist of a saltwater electric eel which is 
double the size and delivers a jolt twice as powerful.
Special Attacks
Electric Jolt. If an electric eel feels threatened or is attacked it 
will discharge a jolt of electricity. This electrical charge causes 
any creature within 5ft of the eel to take 3d8 points of damage, 
all creatures within a 5ft to 10ft range will take 2d8 points of 
damage, and all creatures within 10ft to 15ft take 1d8 hp dam-
age. No saving throw is allowed, though certain magic items 
and spells may grant immunity or reduced damage from the 
electrical attack.
WEED EELS
Weed eels are found in both salt and fresh water, preferring 
depths of around 30ft. Weed eels are so-called because of their 
brownish green colouration, and because they tend to anchor 
themselves in the rocks and dirt of the sea or lake bed, looking 
for all the world like a bed of seaweed. A weed eel lair consists 
of many small tunnels of about 6 inches diameter which even -
tually join into a series of 6ft tunnels, which in turn lead to a 
large cave which is the actual lair. The cave is usually about 600 
square ft and the eels pave the bottom of it with any suitable 
material they find: small stones, gems, coins, and similar-sized 
shiny objects. Any attempt by anyone or anything other than 
a weed eel to enter any hole leading to the lair will provoke an 
immediate attack by every eel in the colony.
Special Attacks
Poison Bite. Weed eels have a single biting attack which injects a 
powerful poison into the wound: the victim must save or die.
EFREET
FREQUENCY  Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1
SIZE Large
ALIGNMENT Lawful evil
MOVE 90ft; 240ft flying
ARMOUR CLASS 2 [18]
HIT DICE 10
MELEE ATTACKS 1 fist (1d8 blunt plus 2d8 fire)
SENSES Standard, Sixth sense 5%
LAIR CHANCE Nil
INTELLIGENCE Very (11-12)
MORALE 95
LOOT Nil
EXPERIENCE 7,000 +12/hp
Efreet are creatures from the Elemental Plane of Fire, and can 
travel the elemental planes (although they usually avoid the 
Elemental Plane of Water), the ethereal or astral planes, and 
the Prime Material Plane. They hate djinn and attack them on 
sight. They are lawful and follow agreements and contracts to 
the letter, if not necessarily the original intention. An agreement 
or contract to serve, if you can persuade or threaten the efreet 
into consenting to one, binds the efreet to obedience for 1,001 
days or until it has granted three wishes, whichever is earlier. 
Once the Efreet’s service is complete, it is freed, and cannot be 
subdued by the same person again.
Efreet are strong and can carry 750 lbs without being encum -
bered.
Immunities and Resistances
Immune to fire.  Efreet ignore fire damage and fire-related ef -
fects.
Special Features
Spell-Like Abilities. Efreet can cast the following spells, without 
material components: 
At Will: detect magic, enlarge (only on the efreet itself), invisi-
bility (only on the efreet itself), polymorph self, wall of fire, Im -
proved phantasmal force.
Gaseous Form.  Efreet can turn themselves to a cloud of lumi -
nous, burning flame. While in this form, the Efreet can fit 
through a gap one inch wide, can’t be restrained, and isn’t af-
fected by being prone.
Three Wishes. Many efreet can cast the wish spell, but only to 
grant another’s wish—they can’t grant wishes for themselves 
or for other efreet. They can use this power to grant up to three 
wishes, and regain spent uses at dawn. Some efreet don’t have 
this power.
Special Attacks
Ranged Attack (Hurl Flame). Efreet can attack with 1 hurled flame 
(3d6 fire damage) with a range of 90ft (not modified by range 
increments).
Unarmed Combat. Efreet can be targeted with grappling or over-
bearing attacks, and under some circumstances they may use 
these tactics. If not fended off, they score an unarmed hit with 
a 2+ on 1d20. Hit Mods: ATK/DEF +10/-10. Overbear Results 
Mods: ATK/DEF +4/-4. Grappling Result Mods: ATK/DEF +2/-2.
Languages
Efreet have limited telepathy. Any creature that understands at 
least one language, understands the efreet; and the efreet can 
understand it. In addition, efreet have their own language, and 
all speak Ignan, the lingua franca of the Elemental Plane of Fire. 
Almost all efreet met on the Prime Material Plane also speak 
Common and many speak other languages as well.
===== PAGE 159 =====

ELEMENTAL

8 HD: 900 +12/hp

8 HD: 900 +12/hp
12 HD: 2,000 +16/hp
16 HD: 3,650 +20/hp
8 HD: 900 +12/hp
12 HD: 2,000 +16/hp
16 HD: 3,650 +20/hp
Elementals are formless (but material) manifestations of a spir-
it from one of the elemental planes of existence, which takes 
shape as a pure and animate form of the element. They are usu-
ally summoned by a spell, but can also form in places of high 
magic or with a close connection to the elemental plane.
Unarmed Combat. Elementals cannot be overborne or grappled, 
and do not usually employ these tactics other than occasionally 
overbearing. 
Language
Each of the elemental planes has its own language. Elementals 
understand the speech of their home plane and the common 
tongue. They can speak only in the tongue of their native planes, 
and even this is only a very rudimentary skill; they do not have 
the same kind of thoughts as most sapient beings, which makes 
communication difficult.
AIR ELEMENTALS
Air Elementals are creatures from the Elemental Plane of Air, 
though they may sometimes be found elsewhere. When en -
countered on the Prime Material Plane, usually as a result of 
summoning magic, they take the form of billowing air, visible 
only because of the dust or vapour blown around by the air col-
umn. 
Immunities and Resistances
Immune to Mundane and +1 Weapons.  This creature is immune 
to piercing, blunt, and slashing damage from weapons and un-
armed attacks, unless the weapon causing that damage has a 
magical bonus of +2 or more.
Special Attacks
Airborne Combat.  The air elemental’s great airborne speed 
makes it a swift and formidable opponent. Against an airborne 
opponent it gains +1 “to hit” and +4 to damage.

===== PAGE 160 =====
 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 159
Whirlwind. Air Elementals may also use a whirlwind attack if so 
commanded. To do this, the Air Elemental must assume the 
form of a small cyclone, which takes one full turn (10 combat 
rounds). Typically, this appears to be 30ft in diameter at the 
top and funnels down to 10ft in diameter at its base; the cone 
extends 20ft in height for every 4 HD the elemental possesses. 
This whirlwind attack lasts for only one round, but any crea -
tures with less than 3 hit dice that find themselves within it are 
slain; creatures with 3 HD or more take 2d8 damage. Should 
the cyclone for some reason be unable to reach its full height, 
only creatures with less than 2HD will suffer immediate death, 
whilst all others take only 1d8 damage.
EARTH ELEMENTALS
Earth Elementals are creatures from the Elemental Plane of 
Earth, though they may sometimes be found elsewhere. When 
encountered on the Prime Material Plane, usually as a result of 
summoning magic, they seem to be vaguely humanoid mounds 
of stone, earth, metal and crystal. Whilst they may have a dis -
cernible head and face, it is usually devoid of expression.
Immunities and Resistances
Immune to Mundane and +1 Weapons.  This creature is immune 
to piercing, blunt, and slashing damage from weapons and un-
armed attacks, unless the weapon causing that damage has a 
magical bonus of +2 or more.
Special Features
Pass Through Stone.  Earth elementals pass through rock and 
earth without hindrance, as they do along the ground, but they 
cannot travel through the air nor in (or over) water unless they 
remain in contact with the earth.
Destroy Structures.  The power of Earth Elementals against 
ground-built structures and fortifications is truly devastating; 
they are capable of reducing small structures to rubble in a 
matter of minutes and even the largest will eventually collapse 
if the Elemental is free to work against it. If need be, this can 
be measured as an automatic 2d4 points of structural damage 
(see “Strongholds”) per round.
Special Attacks
Earth Contact. When an earth elemental attacks opponents who 
are also in contact with the earth, their great limbs strike for 
4d8 points of damage; against foes who are not in contact with 
ground, the Earth Elemental strikes for only 4d6 points of dam-
age.
FIRE ELEMENTALS
Fire Elementals are creatures from the Elemental Plane of Fire, 
though they may sometimes be found elsewhere. When en -
countered on the Prime Material Plane, usually as a result of 
summoning magic, they take the form of a great flickering sheet 
of flame, which occasionally seems to have a somewhat human-
oid shape. 
Fire Elementals may not travel across or enter non-flammable 
liquids; they cannot, for instance, cross broad rivers without the 
aid of a bridge or a similar contrivance, though they can leap 
narrow streams. 
Immunities and Resistances
Immune to Mundane and +1 Weapons.  This creature is immune 
to piercing, blunt, and slashing damage from weapons and un-
armed attacks, unless the weapon causing that damage has a 
magical bonus of +2 or more.
Special Attacks
Fists of Flame.  Those unlucky enough to be struck by the fiery 
appendages of these elementals not only suffer 3d8 points of 
damage, but any combustible items exposed will be set alight if 
they fail a saving throw against magical fire, which is subject to 
a -2 penalty. Creatures capable of innately controlling or oth -
erwise making use of fire are somewhat resistant to the Fire 
Elementals, taking only 3d6 points of damage if struck by one.
 WATER ELEMENTALS
Water Elementals are creatures from the Elemental Plane of 
Water, though they may sometimes be found elsewhere. When 
encountered on the Prime Material Plane, usually as a result of 
summoning magic, they take the form of a great wave of water 
or other liquid, constantly in motion, but retaining an almost 
humanoid shape. Some claim that they have sea green eyes, of 
a sort, and others that they were able to discern a mouth. 
Immunities and Resistances
Immune to Mundane and +1 Weapons.  This creature is immune 
to piercing, blunt, and slashing damage from weapons and un-
armed attacks, unless the weapon causing that damage has a 
magical bonus of +2 or more.
Special Attacks
Melee Combat.  Water Elementals forced to fight on land can -
not do so more than sixty yards from the place at which they 
entered the plane they are currently on, which on the Prime 
Material Plane is generally the place of summoning. Addition -
ally, when fighting on land they strike opponents for only 5d4 
points of damage. If surrounded by water (including marshy 
terrain), they attack for 5d6 blunt damage.
Sink Ships. Ships are particularly vulnerable to Water Elementals, 
which are capable of overturning any vessel with a tonnage 
equal to or less than their HD, and of stopping the movement 
of those with a tonnage equal or less than the Water Elemen -
tal’s hit points. Ships with a greater tonnage than the Elemen -
tal has hit points total can travel at 1% of their speed for each 
point by which tonnage exceeds the elemental’s hit points.
===== PAGE 161 =====
160 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
ELEPHANT
AFRICAN ASIAN MAMMOTH MASTODON
FREQUENCY Common Common Very rare Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d12 1d12 1d12 1d12

SENSES Standard Standard Standard Standard
LAIR CHANCE Nil Nil Nil Nil
INTELLIGENCE Animal (1) Animal (1) Animal (1) Animal (1)
MORALE 100 95 110 105
LOOT Nil Nil Nil Nil
EXPERIENCE 1,400 +14/hp 1,100 +13/hp 2,300 +17/hp 1,900 +16/hp
Elephants are large mammals found in subtropical climates, 
where they roam wide open plains and grasslands in small herds 
or families. They have a tough hoary grey hide, four sturdy legs, 
two large tusks, a great trunk that they use to manipulate their 
surroundings and two very large ears. African elephants are 
typically larger than their Asian cousins and have proportionally 
larger ears, but both are relatively intelligent animals that can 
be trained to work as beasts of burden and obey simple com -
mands and are occasionally even used for warfare. Mammoths 
and mastodons are thought to be the ancestors of the common 
elephant, the principal difference being their great woolly coats, 
which make them better suited to subarctic environments.
In general, elephants are relatively peaceful creatures, but when 
roused they can be devastating. 
Treasure: Ivory is worth 1d6×100 gp per tusk. An elephant tusk 
is hugely encumbering (due to the size as much as the weight).
Special Attacks
Limitation on Multiple Attacks.  No single opponent may be sub -
ject to more than two of an elephant’s attacks at one time, 
though elephants can simultaneously combat as many ene -
mies as it has attacks.
Unarmed Combat. Elephants cannot be overborne or grappled, 
and do not employ these tactics. 
ELF
FREQUENCY Uncommon
NO. ENCOUNTERED 20d10
SIZE Medium
ALIGNMENT Chaotic good
MOVE 120ft
ARMOUR CLASS 5 [15]
HIT DICE 1+1
MELEE ATTACKS By weapon
SENSES Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE 10%
INTELLIGENCE High (13-14)
MORALE 50
LOOT Individual 5 each; lair Hoard 7, 
Cache 5, 6
EXPERIENCE 30 +1/hp
Sometimes elves live in a complex civilisation, other times they 
are found living in wild bands. While there are several different 
types of elves, most do share the following traits:
Elves live in a rather loose society where independent clans 
pledge their support to a central ruler. They will likely place their 
homes in a secluded thicket or meadow.
When encountered in groups of 20 or more there will likely be 
a fighter of 2nd level or better. If there are 40 or more then 
there will probably be a 2nd level fighter / 2nd level magic-user 
or better in the group. If there are 100 or more elves then the 
leader is likely to be a 4th level fighter / 8th level magic-user with 
2 additional 4th level fighters / 5th level magic-users, and a 4th 
level fighter / 4th level magic-user / 4th level cleric. 
===== PAGE 162 =====
 CHAPTER TEN: MONSTER STATISTICS | 161
Typically elves will wear scale, ring, or chain armour and shield. 
They will be usually armed with spears, swords, and bows. High-
er level fighter / magic-users and fighter / magic-user / clerics 
have a 10% chance per level per class to possess some sort of 
usable magic item.
Surprise and Initiative
Surprise. Elves have the ability to move quietly when in natural 
surroundings, and are able to surprise on a roll of 1–4 on a d6 
in a normal, unexpected encounter. 
Undetectable in Certain Surroundings. Elves are able to blend into 
foliage and become virtually invisible, so long as they do not at-
tack. In forested terrain, this allows for undetectable ambush -
es with one round of automatic surprise.
Immunities and Resistances
Resistance to Sleep and Charm.  Elves have a 90% resistance to 
sleep and charm effects.
Special Features
Ancestral Features. Elves have the same ancestral features as a 
player character elf.
Special Attacks
Melee Attack. Elves attack with a +1 “to hit” with longswords.
Ranged Attack. When using any pulled bow elves receive a +1 to 
their attack roll.
Unarmed Combat. For an elf wearing chainmail: If not fended off, 
they score an unarmed hit with a 14+ on 1d20. Hit Mods: ATK/
DEF +6/-4. Overbear Results Mods: ATK/DEF +0/-0. Grappling 
Result Mods: ATK/DEF +0/+0.
Language
Elves have their own language, and can also speak common, 
goblin, orc, hobgoblin, gnoll, halfling, and gnome.
ETTERCAP
FREQUENCY Rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d2
SIZE Medium
ALIGNMENT Neutral evil
MOVE 120ft
ARMOUR CLASS 6
HIT DICE 5+1
MELEE ATTACKS 3 (1d4/1d4/1d8)
SENSES Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE 30%
INTELLIGENCE Semi- (2-4)
MORALE 70
LOOT Nil
EXPERIENCE 150 +5/hp
Ettercaps appear to be humanoid 
arachnids. An ettercap will use its 
silk to prepare traps around its lair, 
such as webs and tripwires, and 
may use a silk-based weapon 
such as a lasso or garrotte. Et -
tercaps can communicate with 
spiders of all types, and they 
are often found working to -
gether with giant spiders.
Special Features
Webs. Ettercaps can spin silk 
from their short, stubby tails 
like a giant spider does. 
These are used to create 
traps around the lair, or 
to snare passers-by in 
ambushes.
Special Attacks
Poison Bite. The ettercap’s bite is venomous. Anyone bitten must 
make a saving throw vs. poison or die.
Unarmed Combat. Ettercaps can be targeted with grappling or 
overbearing attacks, although they don’t use these tactics. If 
not fended off, they score an unarmed hit with a 2+ on 1d20. 
Hit Mods: ATK/DEF +8/-8. Overbear Results Mods: ATK/DEF 
+1/-1. Grappling Result Mods: ATK/DEF +0/-0.
ETTIN
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1d4
SIZE Large (13ft)
ALIGNMENT Chaotic evil
MOVE 120ft
ARMOUR CLASS 3 [17]
HIT DICE 10
MELEE ATTACKS 1 club (2d8) and 1 other club (3d6)
SENSES Infravision 60ft
LAIR CHANCE 20%
INTELLIGENCE Low (5-7)
MORALE 95
LOOT Cache 1 each; Hoard 3 and Cache 11 
in lair
EXPERIENCE 1,370 +14/hp
Ettins are two-headed giants, nocturnal creatures that live be -
low ground. Each of an ettin’s heads controls one arm. The right 
side is slightly dominant, and can cause 3d6 damage whereas 
the left can inflict 2d8. Ettins are seldom surprised because one 
head or the other is usually keeping watch.

===== PAGE 163 =====
162 | OSRIC 3.0 - PART TWO: MONSTERS 
Surprise and Initiative
Surprise. Ettins are only surprised on a 1 in 6.
Unarmed Combat. Ettins can attack and be targeted with grap -
pling or overbearing attacks. If not fended off, they score an 
unarmed hit with a 2+ on 1d20. Hit Mods: ATK/DEF +8/-8. Over-
bear Results Mods: ATK/DEF +4/-4. Grappling Result Mods: 
ATK/DEF +2/-2.
Language
Ettins speak giantish, and each head has a 20% chance to speak 
common.
EXECUTIONER’S HOOD
FREQUENCY Rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1
SIZE Small
ALIGNMENT Neutral evil
MOVE 60ft
ARMOUR CLASS 6 [14]
HIT DICE 3 to 6 hit dice
MELEE ATTACKS 1 (1d4)
SENSES Standard
LAIR CHANCE Nil
INTELLIGENCE Semi- (2-4)
MORALE N/A
LOOT Nil
EXPERIENCE 250 +4/hp
A small, weird monster that may be related to the lurker above, 
the executioner’s hood often dwells underground or in ruins. 
The creature drops from above to cover a victim’s head and 
suffocate them. Because the executioner’s hood actually sur -
rounds its victim’s head, any attack affecting the creature will 
also affect its victim.
The only known ways to remove an executioner’s hood are to 
kill it or to bathe it in alcohol, which will intoxicate it until it be -
comes flaccid and helpless.
Special Attacks
Strangle. The executioner’s hood drops from niches, cracks or 
anchor-points on the ceiling onto its victim’s head and begins 
to strangle. If its first attack is successful, the creature will hit 
automatically each round thereafter, inflicting rolled damage 
(1d4 hp).
Unarmed Combat. Executioner’s hoods cannot be overborne or 
grappled, and do not employ these tactics. 
EYE OF THE DEEP
FREQUENCY Very rare
NO. ENCOUNTERED 1
SIZE Large
ALIGNMENT Lawful evil
MOVE 60ft swimming
ARMOUR CLASS 5
HIT DICE 11
MELEE ATTACKS 2 pincers (2d4) and bite (1d6)
SENSES Standard
LAIR CHANCE 20%
INTELLIGENCE Very (11-12)
MORALE 100
LOOT Cache 4
EXPERIENCE 3,000 +16/hp
The eye of the deep is a fish, globular in shape (about 4ft in di -
ameter) with a massive central eye. Two further eyes on stalks 
sprout from the top of the monster, two huge lobster-like pin -
cers emerge from the side, and it has a gaping maw below. 
Special Attacks
Each eye has a unique power.
Central Eye. The central eye can fire a cone 30ft long and 20ft 
in diameter at the base that stuns all within the area of effect 
for 2d4 rounds (save vs aimed magic items to avoid the effect)
Eyestalks. The left and right eyestalks can cast hold monster and 
hold person respectively, once each per round, at will. The two 
eyestalks together can cast phantasmal force once per round, 
at will, if not used to cast hold spells.
